hook.scenario: Type one wrong digit into a credit card field and it rejects you before any bank is contacted. The card's last digit is a check digit, chosen so the whole number satisfies the Luhn rule (a divisibility-style checksum) - a property a CPU verifies in microseconds.

mental_model.description: Every number system operation breaks down into repeating cycles. Divisibility is simply checking if a number lands perfectly at the '12 o'clock' mark of a specific cycle.

pattern_discovery.setup: Find remainders when powers of 10 are divided by 8.
pattern_discovery.prompt: Since 1000 is divisible by 8, what does that mean for any number ending in the same 3 digits?
pattern_discovery.rule: Since 1000 is perfectly divisible by 8, any multiple of 1000 is too. So to check if a huge number is divisible by 8, just look at its last 3 digits.

formula.statement: Divisibility rules: by 2 -> last digit even. By 3 -> digit sum divisible by 3. By 4 -> last 2 digits divisible by 4. By 5 -> ends in 0 or 5. By 6 -> divisible by both 2 and 3. By 8 -> last 3 digits divisible by 8. By 9 -> digit sum divisible by 9. By 11 -> alternating digit sum (odd positions - even positions) divisible by 11. Unit digit cyclicity: powers of 2 cycle in 4 steps (2,4,8,6), 3 cycles (3,9,7,1), 7 cycles (7,9,3,1), 8 cycles (8,4,2,6). Remainder theorem: x^n / (x+1) -> remainder 1 if n is even, x if n is odd.
formula.explain: Because 10^n is perfectly divisible by 2^n, higher place values do not affect the remainder for powers of 2. For 3 and 9, 10^n always leaves remainder 1, so only the digit sum matters. For unit digits, every power cycle repeats every 4 steps - find exponent mod 4 to find position in cycle.

steps: Unit digits of powers of 7 cycle every 4 steps: 7, 9, 3, 1, 7, 9, 3, 1 ...
53 mod 4 = 1 (since 52 is divisible by 4). So 7^53 has the same unit digit as 7^1 = 7.
Unit digits of powers of 3 cycle every 4 steps: 3, 9, 7, 1, 3, 9, 7, 1 ...
29 mod 4 = 1. So 3^29 has the same unit digit as 3^1 = 3.
Unit digit of 7^53 + 3^29 = unit digit of (7 + 3) = unit digit of 10 = 0.
Divisible by 4? Check last 2 digits: 92. Is 92 divisible by 4? 92 / 4 = 23. Yes.
Divisible by 3? Sum of digits: 8+4+7+3+9+2 = 33. Is 33 divisible by 3? Yes (33 = 3 x 11).
Divisible by 6? Must be divisible by BOTH 2 and 3. Last digit 2 is even (divisible by 2). Digit sum 33 divisible by 3. So yes, divisible by 6.

shortcuts.title: Rule of 11 Alternating Sum
shortcuts.trick: Sum odd place digits, sum even place digits. If the difference is 0 or a multiple of 11, the number is divisible by 11.

shortcuts.title: Unit Digit Cyclicity
shortcuts.trick: Powers of numbers repeat their last digits in cycles of 4. Divide the exponent by 4 to find the unit digit.
shortcuts.example: Unit digit of 2^50: 50 mod 4 = 2. Same as 2^2 = 4. Unit digit is 4.

common_mistakes.title: Ignoring zero as an even number
common_mistakes.explanation: 0 is perfectly divisible by 2. Do not exclude it when counting even digits or applying divisibility rules.

common_mistakes.title: Misapplying the cycle for exponents that are multiples of 4
common_mistakes.explanation: When the exponent mod 4 is 0, the unit digit is the 4th term in the cycle, NOT 1 (which would be 0th power). For example, 2^4 ends in 6, so 2^40 ends in 6.

recall_questions.q: What is the unit digit of 3^101?
recall_questions.answer: 101 mod 4 = 1. So it is the same as 3^1 = 3.
Check last 2 digits: 36 is divisible by 4, so yes.
Check if the number is divisible by both 2 AND 3.

oa_questions.question: If a 9-digit number 985x3678y is divisible by 72, find the value of (4x - 3y).
oa_questions.approach: 72 = 8 x 9. For divisibility by 8: last 3 digits 78y must be divisible by 8 -> y = 4. For divisibility by 9: digit sum = 9+8+5+x+3+6+7+8+4 = 50+x must be divisible by 9 -> x = 4. So 4(4) - 3(4) = 4.

oa_questions.question: What is the remainder when 17^200 is divided by 18?
